title: Replacing seat cushion foam in a sofa
subtitle: Cut and fit new CMHR foam inside a loose removable seat cushion cover
excerpt: How to replace the collapsed foam inside a loose seat cushion, covering removing the old foam from the cover, cutting new CMHR foam to size, wrapping with Dacron to restore the rounded shape, and fitting the foam back into the cover.
sourceNotes: The Practical Upholsterer (1917). Dix and Miller Modern Upholstery (1948). Cassell's Household Guide (upholstery).

glossaryTerms.definition: Combustion-modified high-resilience foam: the standard upholstery foam grade required by UK regulations for any upholstered furniture manufactured or repaired since 1988. CMHR foam passes the smouldering cigarette and match tests. Using non-CMHR foam in a domestic sofa is a fire-safety breach. Available from upholstery suppliers cut to size.

Two measures of foam performance. Density (kg/m³) affects durability: higher density foam lasts longer. ILD (indentation load deflection) measures softness: 25 ILD is soft, 35 ILD is medium, 45 ILD is firm. A seat cushion that has bottomed out was usually too low in density for the load: replace with a foam at 45 kg/m³ density or above.

content.text: A seat cushion that has lost its shape has usually compressed or broken down at its
core. Replacing the foam inside the existing cover gives the sofa another 10 to 15 years of use without re-upholstering. Choose a replacement foam based on
to match the firmness of the original. A Dacron wrap over the foam softens the edges and restores the rounded cushion profile.

CMHR foam cut to the cushion dimensions, at 50 to 75 mm thick for a seat cushion. Order from an upholstery supplier: give the length, width, and depth, and specify medium-firm (35 ILD) for a standard seat or firm (45 ILD) for a cushion that bottomed out quickly. Dacron polyester wadding to wrap the foam, cut to cover the top, front, and sides. A can of foam glue or spray adhesive to bond the Dacron to the foam.

Unzip or open the cushion cover and pull out the old foam. Measure it: length, width, and depth. Note whether the front edge is square or has a rounded nose. Order the new foam to the same dimensions. If the cover zips fully around three sides, the new foam can be slightly larger than the cover (5 to 10 mm per side): the foam compresses into the cover and gives a firmer, fuller look. If the cover has only a short zip, the foam must be a close fit to thread through the opening.
Step 2, trim the foam if needed

If the foam needs trimming, cut it with the utility knife in a single firm stroke. Do not saw back and forth: this leaves a ragged edge. Mark the cut line with a felt pen and use a long steel ruler or batten as a guide for the knife. Trim a bevel off the front bottom edge of the foam if the original had a rounded nose: a 45-degree cut, then sanded or buffed with the hand, reduces the hard corner.
Step 3, wrap the foam in Dacron

Cut a piece of Dacron large enough to cover the top face, the front face, and both side faces of the foam. Spray a light coat of foam adhesive onto the top and sides of the foam. Press the Dacron onto the sprayed surface and smooth it down. Trim the Dacron flush with the back and bottom edges of the foam: the back and bottom go against the sofa frame and do not need wadding. The Dacron wraps the corners of the foam block, which softens the square profile inside the cover.
Step 4, fit the foam into the cover

Open the zip or cover seam fully. Insert one end of the foam block into the cover and feed it in carefully, keeping the Dacron smooth against the foam surface as it slides through the opening. For a tight fit, pull the cover fabric over the foam rather than pushing the foam in: hold the cover open and tug it over the foam block inch by inch. Zip or close the cover when the foam is fully inserted and the corners are square.

A feather-wrapped cushion has a foam core surrounded by a feather pad inside a downproof ticking inner cover. To restore this style, wrap the new foam in a feather pad (available from bedding suppliers) and enclose in a downproof inner cover before fitting the outer cover. Feather-wrap cushions sink and billow in a way that foam-only cushions do not.

Rotate seat cushions weekly so each cushion wears evenly across the set. Stand the cushions on their ends overnight once a month: this allows the foam to recover from the compression of sitting and extends its life. CMHR foam that is stored compressed for long periods develops a permanent set: do not fold cushions for storage.

attrs.symptom: Foam does not slide into the cover evenly: it bunches at one corner
attrs.cause: The foam is slightly too large for the cover, or the Dacron is catching on the interior seams of the cover.
attrs.fix: Remove the foam and spray the Dacron and cover interior lightly with silicone spray to reduce friction. Re-insert slowly from one end, keeping the foam aligned with the long axis of the cover.

attrs.symptom: Cushion looks lumpy or uneven through the cover after fitting
attrs.cause: The Dacron has bunched under the cover, or the foam corners are not sitting in the cover corners.
attrs.fix: Unzip the cover, reposition the foam so the corners align with the cover corners, and smooth the Dacron flat before closing the zip again.
